title: Word Search
code: public bool Exist(char[][] board, string word)
bool Dfs(int r, int c, int k)
if (r < 0 || r >= rows || c < 0 || c >= cols) return false;
bool found = Dfs(r + 1, c, k + 1) || Dfs(r - 1, c, k + 1)

board.label: board — path cells are marked "#" then restored

frames.caption: Goal: does "ABCCED" exist along a chain of orthogonally adjacent cells, no cell reused? Try a DFS from every cell until one spells the word.
frames.note: Word Search is backtracking on a grid: the path is the recursion stack and the mutable state to undo is a board cell we mark visited. We launch a DFS from each cell; the first start whose DFS returns true wins. Worst case is O(m·n·4^L) for L = word length, but pruning on mismatch usually cuts it to almost nothing.

frames.caption: Outer loop reaches (0,0)='A' = word[0]='A'. Launch Dfs(0,0,0).
frames.note: The double for-loop tries each cell as a starting point. We only bother launching a DFS where the cell already matches the first letter — every other start would mismatch and return false on its first call anyway.

frames.caption: (0,0)='A' matches word[0]='A'. MARK it "#" and explore neighbors for 'B'.
frames.note: board[r][c] equals word[k], so this cell can extend the path. We MARK it visited by overwriting it with the sentinel "#" (no separate bool[,] needed) and recurse to match the next letter. Overwriting doubles as the visited check: "#" never equals a real word character, so we can never step back onto our own path.

frames.caption: Recurse into the 4 neighbors of (0,0) — down, up, right, left — stopping at the first that succeeds.
frames.note: A match recurses into the 4 orthogonal neighbors in a fixed order — down, up, right, left — short-circuiting on the first that returns true (||). Only adjacent cells are tried, so the path is always a connected chain; diagonals are never word-adjacent.

frames.caption: (1,0): 'S' != 'B'. Mismatch, so this branch is dead — return false.
frames.note: board[r][c] does NOT equal word[k] (or the cell is out of bounds / already "#"). This branch can never spell the word, so we prune it immediately — return false without descending. Cheap top-of-call checks like this are what keep the 4^L tree from exploding.

frames.caption: (-1,0) is off the board — out of bounds. Prune: return false.
frames.note: board[r][c] does NOT equal word[k] (or the cell is out of bounds / already "#"). This branch can never spell the word, so we prune it immediately — return false without descending. Cheap top-of-call checks like this are what keep the 4^L tree from exploding.

frames.caption: (1,2): already on the path ("#"). Mismatch, so this branch is dead — return false.
frames.note: board[r][c] does NOT equal word[k] (or the cell is out of bounds / already "#"). This branch can never spell the word, so we prune it immediately — return false without descending. Cheap top-of-call checks like this are what keep the 4^L tree from exploding.

frames.caption: (2,3): 'E' != 'D'. Mismatch, so this branch is dead — return false.
frames.note: board[r][c] does NOT equal word[k] (or the cell is out of bounds / already "#"). This branch can never spell the word, so we prune it immediately — return false without descending. Cheap top-of-call checks like this are what keep the 4^L tree from exploding.

frames.caption: (2,1)='D' matches word[5]='D'. MARK it "#" and explore neighbors for '∎'.
frames.note: board[r][c] equals word[k], so this cell can extend the path. We MARK it visited by overwriting it with the sentinel "#" (no separate bool[,] needed) and recurse to match the next letter. Overwriting doubles as the visited check: "#" never equals a real word character, so we can never step back onto our own path.

frames.caption: Recurse into the 4 neighbors of (2,1) — down, up, right, left — stopping at the first that succeeds.
frames.note: A match recurses into the 4 orthogonal neighbors in a fixed order — down, up, right, left — short-circuiting on the first that returns true (||). Only adjacent cells are tried, so the path is always a connected chain; diagonals are never word-adjacent.

frames.caption: k == 6 = word.Length: the whole word "ABCCED" is matched. Return true.
frames.note: k reached word.Length: every letter has been matched along a connected, non-reused path. The recursion returns true and the || chain carries that true all the way up, so the outer loop stops launching new starts. Answer: the word exists.

frames.caption: Dfs returned true: the cells (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2)→(2,1) spell "ABCCED". Exist returns true.
frames.note: Done. The winning path is a connected chain of 6 distinct cells, each marked "#" on entry and restored as the stack unwinds — by the time we return, the board is back to its original letters. Space is O(L) for the recursion depth; no extra visited array was ever allocated.
